title: Cyrus sends the exiles home
summary_text: Cyrus of Persia announces that the LORD has charged him to rebuild the temple in Jerusalem. The exiles are invited to return, and their neighbors provide silver, gold, goods, and animals for the journey. The chapter sets restoration in motion by pairing imperial permission with God's own stirring of the people.
key_points: Cyrus issues a return decree.
Temple vessels are restored.
Neighbors help fund the journey.
God stirs the people to go up.
evidence_phrases: the LORD stirred up the spirit
let him go up to Jerusalem
the people of Judah and Benjamin

title: The first returnees are listed
summary_text: The chapter records the families, priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, and servants who returned from exile. The list is long because the restoration is being built from remembered names and covenant families. It shows that return is not only geographic but also communal and ordered.
key_points: The returning families are carefully counted.
Priests and Levites are included in the list.
Temple servants and singers also return.
Restoration begins with remembered identity.

title: The altar and the foundation
summary_text: The returned exiles rebuild the altar first so that sacrifice can resume before the temple itself is finished. They lay the foundation of the house with praise, but the joy is mixed with grief from those who remember the former temple. The chapter captures both hope and sorrow at the beginning of restoration.
key_points: The altar is rebuilt first.
Daily offerings resume.
The temple foundation is laid with praise.
Older worshipers weep while others rejoice.
evidence_phrases: the people shouted with a loud shout

title: Opposition halts the rebuilding
summary_text: Local opponents first offer false help and then work to discourage the returned exiles from rebuilding. Under later Persian kings, accusations and political pressure stop the temple work for a time. The chapter shows how external resistance can slow restoration without canceling God's promise.
key_points: The enemies try to join the work falsely.
Their opposition becomes political pressure.
The rebuilding is stopped by imperial order.
Restoration is delayed but not destroyed.
evidence_phrases: the work of the house of God
the letter was read before them

title: Haggai and Zechariah restart the work
summary_text: The prophets Haggai and Zechariah stir the people to rebuild, and the work begins again despite past resistance. Tattenai investigates the project, but the elders explain that Cyrus had authorized it. The chapter highlights prophetic courage and obedient action under scrutiny.
key_points: The prophets restart the rebuilding.
The work proceeds under scrutiny.
Tattenai asks for the legal basis.
The elders point to Cyrus's decree.

title: Darius confirms and the temple is finished
summary_text: Darius searches the records, confirms Cyrus's decree, and orders the temple project to continue without interference. The house is completed and dedicated with sacrifice, joy, and Passover celebration. The chapter closes the rebuilding story with formal approval and renewed worship.
key_points: The records confirm Cyrus's decree.
The temple is completed.
Dedication offerings are made.
Passover resumes with restored worship.
evidence_phrases: let the work of this house of God alone
the hand of the God of Israel

title: Ezra arrives with the king's commission
summary_text: Ezra comes from Babylon with royal authorization to teach and administer the law of God in Jerusalem. The letter from Artaxerxes provides support, protection, and resources, while Ezra is described as a skilled scribe devoted to the law. The chapter shifts the story from temple rebuilding to covenant teaching.
key_points: Ezra travels with royal backing.
He is skilled in the law of Moses.
The king funds and protects his mission.
Jerusalem receives a new teacher of the law.

title: Ezra's journey with fasting and treasure
summary_text: Ezra gathers the returning company, lists the gifts entrusted to the temple, and leads the group in a fast at the river. He refuses a military escort because he has testified that God's hand is for good on those who seek him. The journey becomes a living demonstration of dependence and trust.
key_points: The returning caravan is organized carefully.
Temple gifts are weighed out and entrusted.
Ezra proclaims a fast for safety.
The journey is made under God's protection.
evidence_phrases: we fasted and begged our God

title: Ezra confesses the people's mixed marriages
summary_text: Ezra is devastated to learn that many leaders and people have taken foreign wives and crossed the line into covenant unfaithfulness. He prays with grief, confessing that the nation has again ignored God's commands despite mercy from exile. The chapter is a solemn call to repentance before the holy God.
key_points: Ezra hears of the mixed marriages.
He tears his clothes and prays.
He confesses the nation's guilt.
Grace from exile heightens the seriousness of the sin.

title: The people repent and put away the foreign wives
summary_text: The people gather in trembling repentance and agree to address the sin Ezra has named. A careful process follows to examine the cases and send away the foreign wives, with named leaders overseeing the matter. The chapter ends Ezra with a painful but concrete act of covenant cleansing.
key_points: The assembly acknowledges the sin.
Leaders oversee the corrective process.
Cases are examined one by one.
The foreign marriages are dealt with.
